behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise tournament: Unit beats Integration because this behavior is a local backoff-calculation rule (failure count + cap) with no required module handoff in the behavior text. Unit then beats System because no real OS/network/subprocess is needed; a pure function/object test catches the real failure. Rejection gate: Integration/System were considered and rejected because the behavior text only specifies delay math and cap enforcement, not cross-module or real-infra effects.
behaviors.test_description: Test the backoff calculator directly with consecutive failure indices and a configured max; assert exponential sequence and clamp-at-max behavior.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise tournament: Integration beats Unit because failure can occur at the boundary between response interpretation and retry policy application (status/body/headers mapped into delay decision). Integration then beats System because real HTTP is not required to trigger this failure; in-process stubbed responses are sufficient. Rejection gate: System was considered and rejected because behavior text does not require real network timing/socket behavior, only correct classification + wait computation.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry controller + response classifier + backoff policy, feed synthetic rate-limit/non-rate-limit responses (including retry-after metadata), and assert retry decision plus computed delay.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise tournament: Integration beats Unit because correctness depends on interaction among timer/scheduler, retry orchestration, and transport call replay. Integration then beats System because a fake clock plus in-process transport stub can reproduce resend semantics without real HTTP/OS timing primitives. Rejection gate: System was considered and rejected since behavior text requires resend transparency, not real network failure modes.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake time and a stub transport that first returns rate-limit then success; assert the same request payload is resent after the expected delay and final success is returned to caller without extra wrapper leakage.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise tournament: Integration beats Unit because the failure typically appears when retry loop termination and error-mapping layers interact. Integration then beats System because this can be forced via deterministic in-process failing responses; no real OS/network dependency is required. Rejection gate: System was considered and rejected because behavior text focuses on typed error mapping at retry exhaustion, not real infra behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Drive the retry pipeline with repeated rate-limit failures until max retries is reached; assert thrown/rejected error is exactly `QuotaExhaustedError` with expected typed fields, and not a generic transport error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise tournament: Integration beats Unit because this behavior is explicitly cross-module end-to-end within the library (retry state + backoff + quota tracking). Integration then beats System because loop-termination failures can be exposed with controlled in-process stubs and fake time, without real subprocess/network. Rejection gate: System was considered and rejected since no behavior text requires real OS timers or live HTTP conditions to prove termination.
behaviors.test_description: Run a bounded retry scenario with perpetual rate-limit responses under fake clock; assert attempt count reaches configured limit, control flow exits, and error surfaces within deterministic simulated time (no unbounded pending tasks).

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise tournament: Integration beats Unit because the failure boundary is shared mutable state across concurrent callers/modules, not a single function. Integration then beats System because same-process async concurrency is reproducible in-process; real network/OS is not required to validate shared bucket accounting. Rejection gate: System was considered and rejected because behavior text scopes to callers in the same process and shared in-library quota state.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/quota manager, fire concurrent `call()` requests from multiple async tasks, and assert aggregate token/attempt consumption is globally enforced (not per-caller isolated).

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise tournament: Integration beats Unit because FIFO correctness depends on queueing + dispatcher coordination over multiple requests. Integration then beats System because dispatch-order bugs can be caught with in-process scheduler/transport fakes; no real HTTP/OS dependency is required. Rejection gate: System was considered and rejected because behavior text is about ordering policy inside the library, not infrastructure-specific transport behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Queue multiple blocked requests with known submission order, release send capacity stepwise, and assert observed dispatch order matches original enqueue order under concurrent async load.
